ocr.text: The analysis of variation in diagnostic performance metrics is crucial for understanding the reliability and effectiveness of
different feature types in medical assessments. The data presented in the table categorizes variations into three distinct types:
Special cause variation (low), Common cause variation, and Special cause variation (high). Notably, the column labeled
"Sensitivity (%)" indicates how effectively a test correctly identifies true positives, with the "Total" column summarizing the

overall performance across feature types, including brain and tonsil samples. 

In reviewing the special cause variation (low)
data, it is evident that certain feature types, such as the AAC designation, exhibit varying levels of sensitivity, with a recorded
value of 57.26%.
Observed mortality rate  Risk Adjusted mortality rate
Special cause variation (low)  Common cause variation  Special cause variation (high)  Total
Special cause variation (low)  2  4  0  6
Common cause variation  0  281  6  287
Special cause variation (high)  0  3  2  5
Feature type  Sensitivity(%)  Specificity(%)  Accuracy(%)  MCC  Precision (%)  ER(%)
Designation  day  brain  tonsil  Lymph node  heart  liver  spleen  lung  kidney  stomach
This finding suggests that while some diagnostic methods may be robust, others require further optimization to enhance their
accuracy and reliability. Additionally, the specificity percentage of 63.89% highlights the importance of considering both
sensitivity and specificity in assessing diagnostic tools, as these metrics collectively influence clinical decision-making and
patient outcomes.
